variable: 심박변이(HRV) — overall autonomic marker vs hypertension
what_it_measures: Beat-to-beat variation in R-R intervals from ECG; a noninvasive quantitative index of cardiac autonomic (sympathetic + parasympathetic/vagal) modulation of the sinoatrial node. Higher HRV = greater vagal/parasympathetic tone and autonomic flexibility; lower HRV = sympathovagal imbalance.
bp_relationship: INVERSE and bidirectional. Lower HRV is both a CONSEQUENCE of (cross-sectional, lower in prevalent hypertensives) and a PREDICTOR/ANTECEDENT of higher BP and incident hypertension. Mechanism: reduced vagal tone + relative sympathetic dominance -> impaired baroreflex BP buffering -> sustained sympathetic overactivity -> elevated BP.
references: Schroeder EB, Liao D, Chambless LE, et al. 2003, Hypertension (ARIC Study) 42(6):1106-1114. Cohort 11,061 (age 45-64); cross-sectionally HRV (SDNN, RMSSD, R-R) lower in hypertensives across the FULL BP range after adjustment. Prospectively among 7,099 normotensives at baseline, low HRV predicted incident hypertension over ~9 yr: lowest vs highest quartile HR 1.24 (95% CI 1.10-1.40) for SDNN, 1.36 (1.21-1.54) for RMSSD, 1.44 (1.27-1.63) for R-R interval. Establishes the temporal sequence: low HRV precedes hypertension.

Singh JP, Larson MG, Tsuji H, et al. 1998, Hypertension (Framingham Heart Study) 32(2):293-297. 931 men + 1111 women, first 2h ambulatory ECG. Cross-sectionally HRV significantly lower in hypertensive men and women. Prospectively (4-yr follow-up, 633 men/801 women normotensive at baseline; 119 men/125 women became hypertensive): low LF power predicted new-onset hypertension in MEN (not women); in men LF was a stronger predictor than BMI. Sex-specific direction.

Liao D, Cai J, Barnes RW, et al. 1996, Am J Hypertens (ARIC) 9(12):1147-1156. n=2,061. Graded inverse association between baseline high-frequency (HF, vagal) power and incident hypertension: adjusted odds ratios from highest to lowest HF quartile 1.00, 1.46, 1.50, 2.44 (lowest quartile 95% CI 1.15-5.20). Lower vagal activity -> higher hypertension risk.

variable: Time-domain HRV (SDNN, RMSSD, pNN50, R-R interval)
what_it_measures: SDNN = standard deviation of normal-to-normal R-R intervals (overall HRV / total autonomic variability). RMSSD and pNN50 = short-term beat-to-beat differences, predominantly reflecting parasympathetic/vagal tone. Higher values = greater vagal modulation.
bp_relationship: INVERSE. SDNN, RMSSD and R-R interval are lower in hypertensives than normotensives and lower values predict incident hypertension. SDNN is the most consistently/significantly reduced; RMSSD/pNN50 reduced but sometimes not statistically significant in smaller studies.
references: Schroeder EB et al. 2003, Hypertension (ARIC): crude SDNN, RMSSD, and R-R lower in hypertensives vs normotensives, persisting after adjustment (age, sex, race, center, diabetes, smoking, education, BMI). Lowest-quartile hazard ratios for incident hypertension — SDNN 1.24, RMSSD 1.36, R-R 1.44 (see above).

ELSA-Brasil — Almeida-Santos / Brazilian Longitudinal Study of Adult Health, 2021, J Hum Hypertens (Nature) — prospective, 7,665 normotensive at baseline, 4-yr follow-up. Low values of HRV indices (SDNN, VLF, LF) significantly associated with increased relative risk of incident hypertension after full adjustment; even normal-BP (not just prehypertensive) subjects with low SDNN/VLF/LF were at increased risk. Conclusion: cardioautonomic disturbance precedes clinical hypertension.

Comparative cross-sectional studies (e.g., normotensive vs hypertensive Indian adults, ScienceDirect S0019483223000457): time-domain SDNN and NN50 significantly lower (p<0.05) in hypertensives; RMSSD and pNN50 lower but often non-significant.

variable: Frequency-domain HRV (LF power, HF power, LF/HF ratio)
what_it_measures: HF (0.15-0.40 Hz) = parasympathetic/vagal activity. LF (0.04-0.15 Hz) = mixed sympathetic + parasympathetic, modulated by baroreflex. LF/HF ratio historically interpreted as 'sympathovagal balance' (sympathetic dominance when elevated).
bp_relationship: INVERSE for HF (lower vagal power -> higher BP/hypertension risk). For LF/HF: hypertensives typically show HIGHER LF and HIGHER LF/HF ratio (relative sympathetic dominance). Low HF predicts incident hypertension; low LF predicts new-onset hypertension in men (Framingham).
references: Liao D et al. 1996, Am J Hypertens (ARIC): graded inverse HF-to-incident-hypertension association, lowest HF quartile OR 2.44 (1.15-5.20).
Singh JP et al. 1998, Hypertension (Framingham): low LF predicted incident hypertension in men.

HRV in hypertension reviews/cross-sectional (e.g., PMC7582062; MDPI Diagnostics 2023 13(4):785 'Role of HRV in Different Hypertensive Syndromes'): SDNN and pNN50 lower, LF and LF/HF ratio higher in hypertensives — indicating sympathetic overactivity + vagal withdrawal.

CAVEAT on LF/HF: Billman GE 2013, Front Physiol 4:26, and Reyes del Paso et al. — the LF/HF ratio does NOT accurately quantify cardiac sympathovagal balance; LF reflects baroreflex modulation rather than pure sympathetic tone. Interpret LF/HF as a proxy, not ground truth.

variable: Baroreflex sensitivity (BRS) — mechanistic link between HRV and BP
what_it_measures: BRS = magnitude of reflex change in heart rate / R-R interval per unit change in arterial BP (ms/mmHg). An integrated measure of autonomic (both sympathetic and parasympathetic) short-term BP regulation; mechanistically couples HRV to BP control.
bp_relationship: INVERSE with BP. Reduced/blunted BRS accompanies and precedes hypertension; impaired baroreflex buffering -> wider BP variability and sustained sympathetic overactivity -> elevated BP. BRS is positively correlated with HRV and inversely with BP variability. Hypertension also RESETS the baroreflex to a higher pressure with reduced sensitivity (reverse causation).
references: AHA Hypertension — 'Baroreflex Sensitivity Inversely Correlates With Ambulatory Blood Pressure in Healthy Normotensive Humans' (ahajournals.org HYPERTENSIONAHA.107.090308): lower BRS associated with higher ambulatory BP even within normotensive range.

Crisafulli A et al. 2022, 'Hypertension depresses arterial baroreflex control of heart rate and cardiac output during rest, exercise, and metaboreflex activation' (PMC9602692): hypertensives have blunted baroreflex control of heart period; baroreflex reset to higher pressure at cost of reduced sensitivity.

Reviews on baroreflex/autonomic mechanism of hypertension: BRS is a sensitive integrated index of sympathetic+parasympathetic regulation; in hypertensives BRS is positively related to HRV and inversely related to BP variability, so impaired baroreflex -> wider BP swings (Springer 'Heart Rate Variability, Blood Pressure Variability: What Is Their Significance in Hypertension', 2022).

variable: HRV as predictor of broader cardiovascular outcomes (context for BP pathway)
what_it_measures: Same HRV indices used as prognostic markers for first cardiovascular events and mortality in populations free of overt CVD — contextualizes the strength/effect size of low HRV as a risk marker on the hypertension-CVD continuum.
bp_relationship: INVERSE/predictive but MODEST effect size. Low HRV signals autonomic dysregulation that drives both hypertension and downstream CVD; pooled risk roughly 1.3-1.5x lowest-vs-highest HRV. Supports HRV as an antecedent marker rather than a strong standalone predictor.
references: Hillebrand S, Gast KB, de Mutsert R, et al. 2013, Europace 15(5):742-749 — meta-analysis & dose-response meta-regression of HRV and first CV event in populations without known CVD: pooled RR (lowest vs highest SDNN) 1.35 (95% CI 1.10-1.67); LF 1.45 (1.12-1.87); HF 1.32 (0.96-1.81). A 1-SD lower SDNN ~ 32-45% higher CV risk.

General meta-analytic finding cited across reviews: lower HRV associated with higher all-cause death / CV events in CVD patients (pooled HR for all-cause death ~2.27) — larger effect in diseased populations vs primary-prevention cohorts.

what_it_measures: Percent of hemoglobin saturated with oxygen (pulse oximetry). Proxies for hypoxia/desaturation include nocturnal SpO2 dips, time below 90% (T90), oxygen desaturation index (ODI), and 'hypoxic burden' (area under the desaturation curve). Lower SpO2 = hypoxia.
bp_relationship: INVERSE in the clinically dominant range: lower SpO2 / more hypoxia → higher BP. Mechanism: hypoxia activates the carotid-body peripheral chemoreflex → increased sympathetic (muscle sympathetic nerve activity) outflow, RAAS/renin-angiotensin-aldosterone activation, reduced nitric-oxide bioavailability, endothelial dysfunction, oxidative stress and systemic inflammation → vasoconstriction and sustained BP elevation. Intermittent hypoxia (as in OSA) is especially hypertensinogenic, producing nocturnal/resistant hypertension and abnormal BP variability. CAVEAT: relationship is non-linear/context-dependent — very brief or mild acute hypoxia may change BP little, and some chronic high-altitude adaptation or RAAS-inhibition models can lower BP — but the prevailing direction in OSA/desaturation literature is hypoxia→higher BP.
references: Peppard et al. 2000, N Engl J Med (Wisconsin Sleep Cohort) — dose-response, BMI-independent: baseline AHI≥15 vs 0 gave ~4-year adjusted OR ≈2.89 (95% CI 1.46-5.64) for incident hypertension; AHI≥15 ~3.2-fold higher odds vs no OSA

Tamisier et al. 2011, Am J Physiol Heart Circ Physiol — controlled chronic intermittent hypoxia 9h/night x28 nights in healthy young adults: diastolic BP rose 71±1.3→74±1.7 mmHg (P<0.01); MSNA rose 9.94→14.63 bursts/min (P<0.05); demonstrates causal hypoxia→BP+sympathetic link

Azarbarzin et al. 2019, Eur Heart J (Sleep Heart Health Study + MrOS) — 'hypoxic burden' (area under desaturation curve) predicts CVD mortality better than AHI; highest-burden quintiles HR ≈1.81 and 2.73 (MrOS), ≈1.96 (SHHS, 95% CI 1.11-3.43)

Hansen/Hainsworth & high-altitude microneurography studies — sympathetic nerve activity markedly elevated at altitude vs sea level (~48 vs 16 bursts/min) with MAP rising ~77→87 mmHg; chronic hypoxia increases noradrenaline spillover and BP in healthy humans

OSA–hypertension reviews/meta-analyses 2018-2024 (e.g., Hypertension Research, Front Med) — intermittent hypoxia/sympathetic activation as principal mechanism; OSA associated with resistant and nocturnal hypertension, dose-response with severity

what_it_measures: Number of breaths per minute (general-population median ~15.8/min, KORA-FF4; ~12-20 normal range). Also encompasses breathing pattern (paced vs spontaneous) and respiration-driven beat-to-beat BP oscillations (respiratory sinus arrhythmia).
bp_relationship: BIDIRECTIONAL / pattern-dependent. (a) SLOW breathing lowers BP: paced breathing ~6 breaths/min raises arterial baroreflex sensitivity and vagal tone while lowering sympathetic and chemoreflex drive → reduced SBP/DBP. (b) HIGH respiratory rate signals sympathetic activation / acute physiological stress and is an independent predictor of deterioration and mortality (often stronger than BP or pulse), implying association with adverse cardiovascular state. (c) MECHANISTIC coupling: respiration generates short-term BP oscillations via respiratory sinus arrhythmia and the baroreflex — BP falls during inhalation and rises during exhalation; faster breathing produces smaller respiratory BP swings. Net: slow/regular breathing ↔ lower BP & higher baroreflex sensitivity; fast/irregular breathing ↔ sympathetic activation & higher risk.
references: Joseph et al. 2005, Hypertension 46:714-718 — slow breathing 6/min in essential hypertensives: SBP 149.7±3.7→141.1±4 (P<0.05), DBP 82.7±3→77.8±3.7 mmHg (P<0.01); baroreflex sensitivity rose 5.8±0.7→10.3±2.0 ms/mmHg (hypertensives) and 10.9→16.0 (controls); reduced sympathetic/chemoreflex activation

Mahtani et al. 2012, J Hum Hypertens (RESPeRATE device meta-analysis, 8 trials, n=494) — SBP −3.67 mmHg (95% CI −5.99 to −1.39, P=0.002), DBP −2.51 mmHg (−4.15 to −0.87, P=0.003); BUT effect null after excluding manufacturer-linked trials

Freitas Gonçalves et al. 2022, Health Science Reports / Eur Heart J suppl — broader meta-analysis (22 studies, n=17,214): device-guided slow breathing did NOT significantly reduce BP vs usual care (SBP MD −2.13, 95% CI −12.71 to 8.44) — tempers efficacy claims

Fieselmann et al. 1993, J Gen Intern Med — respiratory rate >27/min is the single best predictor of in-hospital cardiopulmonary arrest; RR remained predictive after adjustment while pulse and BP were not — RR as superior risk marker
Cretikos et al. 2008, Med J Aust ('Respiratory rate: the neglected vital sign') — elevated RR independent marker of mortality (pneumonia, ED, MEWS/CURB-65 components)

Eckberg / respiratory sinus arrhythmia & baroreflex literature (e.g., Hypertension, Am J Physiol) — RSA arises from conversion of respiratory BP variability into HR variability via the vagal baroreflex; BP decreases on inhalation/increases on exhalation; faster breathing yields smaller respiratory BP oscillations (frequency-dependent)

what_it_measures: Beats per minute; with stroke volume determines cardiac output (CO = HR x SV), one of the two multiplicative determinants of BP (BP = CO x TPR). Reflects net autonomic balance (sympathetic vs parasympathetic/vagal tone) on the sinoatrial node.
bp_relationship: Context-dependent (this is the critical modeling caveat). INVERSE at rest on a beat-to-beat basis via the arterial baroreflex: an acute BP rise activates baroreceptors -> reflex parasympathetic activation + sympathetic inhibition -> HR, contractility, stroke volume, and vascular resistance fall (negative feedback keeping BP constant); a BP fall does the opposite. POSITIVE/parallel during exercise because the baroreflex resets upward intensity-dependently and central command + exercise pressor reflex drive HR and BP up together to increase CO. POSITIVE across individuals chronically: higher tonic HR co-occurs with higher BP because both are downstream of shared sympathetic overdrive.
references: Baroreflex Sensitivity Inversely Correlates With Ambulatory Blood Pressure in Healthy Normotensive Humans, Hypertension (AHA, ahajournals.org/doi/10.1161/HYPERTENSIONAHA.107.090308) — baroreflex enforces inverse SBP-HR relation; baroreflex sensitivity inversely correlates with ambulatory BP in normotensives.

Human/animal baroreflex reviews (PMC9602692; Fadel et al., PMC3253263 'Human Investigations into the Arterial and Cardiopulmonary Baroreflexes during Exercise') — during exercise the arterial baroreflex resets in direct proportion to intensity, allowing parallel rises in BP and HR to raise cardiac output; exercise pressor/metaboreflex raises BP via increased HR, contractility (Emax), and CO.

Mechanistic statement: BP = CO x TPR, CO = HR x SV; rapid beat-to-beat BP regulation occurs primarily via baroreflex control of CO through rapid HR changes (PMC2343007, baroreflex CO/vascular conductance during exercise).

what_it_measures: Heart rate measured at rest (typically seated/supine, awake, quiescent). A clinical marker of resting sympathetic tone / autonomic balance and a recommended item in hypertension assessment (ESH guidelines). Higher RHR indicates sympathetic overdrive.
bp_relationship: POSITIVE and independent as a long-term cross-individual predictor: elevated RHR precedes and predicts incident hypertension and higher BP. Mechanism = chronic sympathetic overactivity that raises both HR and BP (and hematocrit/metabolic abnormalities). RHR >80 bpm flagged as marker of pronounced sympathetic overdrive with adverse outcomes. (Note: this long-term positive risk association is distinct from the acute within-person baroreflex inverse relation.)
references: Wang et al. (or cohort authors), 'Association of Resting Heart Rate With Blood Pressure and Incident Hypertension Over 30 Years in Black and White Adults: The CARDIA Study,' Hypertension, Sept 2020 (AHA, HYPERTENSIONAHA.120.15233; PMC7430042) — n=3888 (1615 men, 2273 women) followed ~30 yrs; HR per +10 bpm RHR = 1.47 (1.23-1.75) Black men, 1.51 (1.28-1.78) White men, 1.48 (1.26-1.73) White women, 1.02 (0.89-1.17, NS) Black women.

Dose-response meta-analysis of cohort studies (PMC7478507) — linear positive association; ~9% higher incident-HTN RR per +10 bpm; RHR ~100 vs 55.5 bpm = RR 1.58 (1.32-1.88); no significant association in women subgroup.

Dose-response meta-analysis, RHR and risk of hypertension and heart failure (PubMed 29176508) — +10 bpm RHR -> hypertension RR 1.11 (1.07-1.15) [also reported 1.09, 95% CI 1.06-1.13]; linear positive trend.

Palatini P., 'High Heart Rate and Hypertension: Two Intertwined Cardiovascular Risk Factors,' J Clin Hypertension 2025 (Wiley; PMC11804763) and 'High Heart Rate, Sympathetic Overdrive, and CV Risk in Hypertension,' Am J Cardiol 2025 (S0002-9149(25)00576-4) — HR >80 bpm reflects sympathetic overactivity; drives BP, organ damage (LVH, endothelial/arterial dysfunction, renal damage); RHR recommended in ESH assessment.

Palatini & Julius, 'Relationship of Tachycardia With High Blood Pressure and Metabolic Abnormalities,' Hypertension 1997 (01.hyp.30.5.1267) — tachycardia clusters with high BP and metabolic abnormalities via sympathetic overactivity.

variable: 걷기 심박수 (Walking / activity-elevated Heart Rate) — incl. heart-rate reserve & HR-per-step
what_it_measures: Heart rate during ambulation/walking or submaximal exertion, i.e. HR above resting under a physical-activity load. Captures the chronotropic (autonomic) response to demand. Often normalized as Heart-Rate Reserve (HRpeak - HRrest) or, with wearables, as daily heart-rate-per-step (chronotropic responsiveness/fitness index).
bp_relationship: Two layers. (a) WITHIN an activity bout: POSITIVE co-movement — walking raises HR and BP together (baroreflex reset + exercise pressor reflex). (b) As a TRAIT predictor of hypertension, the normalized response matters and direction can flip: HIGHER heart-rate reserve / good chronotropic competence is PROTECTIVE against incident HTN (better fitness, lower resting sympathetic tone), whereas a STEEP BP-rise-per-HR-increment slope and an exaggerated exercise BP response predict FUTURE hypertension. So absolute walking HR co-moves positively with BP acutely, but a blunted HR reserve or a steep BP/HR slope flags higher hypertension risk.
references: Sharashova et al., 'Association of exercise heart rate response and incidence of hypertension in men,' (PubMed 24974261) — higher HR reserve protective: HR 0.84 (95% CI 0.74-0.95) for highest vs lowest quartile of HR reserve for incident hypertension in men.

Singh JP et al., 'Blood Pressure Response to Heart Rate During Exercise Test and Risk of Future Hypertension,' Hypertension 2002 (AHA hy0302.105777; PubMed 11897759) — Framingham-related; n~1033 normotensive men, mean age ~43; steeper BP-response-to-HR slope (and percentile curves via 3rd-order polynomial regression) predicts future hypertension; exaggerated exercise BP response RR ~3.8 (2.3-6.1) for incident HTN.

Lauer MS et al., 'Impaired heart rate response to graded exercise: prognostic implications of chronotropic incompetence in the Framingham Heart Study,' Circulation 1996 (PubMed 8608620) — blunted chronotropic response (low HR reserve) predicts mortality and CHD; establishes HR reserve as autonomic/fitness marker.

Occupational activity ambulatory study (PMC9421794) — within-day positive coupling: lifting workdays vs not showed +9.10 bpm HR alongside higher ambulatory SBP (+3.77 mmHg) and DBP (+1.37 mmHg), demonstrating concurrent HR-BP rise with activity.

'Daily Heart Rate per Step: A Wearables Metric Associated With Cardiovascular Disease,' All of Us Research Program (PMC12184247) — wearable HR-per-step (chronotropic responsiveness during walking) associates with CVD; supports walking-HR-derived features for cardiovascular/BP risk modeling.

variable: Wearable wrist HR / PPG as a BP estimator (cross-cut, supports HR variables above)
what_it_measures: Wrist photoplethysmography (PPG) heart rate and pulse-wave morphology used to estimate BP via pulse-wave analysis (PWA) and/or pulse arrival time (PAT), typically with ML models and (often) a cuff-based calibration plus demographic features (~100 features).
bp_relationship: HR alone is a WEAK standalone BP proxy; predictive value comes from combining HR with PPG waveform morphology, PAT, and demographics. Best cuffless wristbands report SBP/DBP mean errors near +/-2.5 to 3.7 mmHg seated, but accuracy degrades by posture (SD up to ~12.5 mmHg standing) and overall accuracy remains regulatorily uncertain. Implication for a BP-estimation model: include resting HR (level/risk), activity-context HR (co-moves with BP), HR reserve / HR-per-step (fitness/autonomic responsiveness), plus PPG-derived morphology.
references: Evaluation of a novel cuffless PPG-based wristband for measuring blood pressure per regulatory standards, Eur Heart J Digital Health 2024 (academic.oup.com/ehjdh/article/5/3/335; PMC11104472) — algorithm errors ~+/-3.7 (SD 4.4) mmHg SBP and +/-2.5 (SD 3.7) mmHg DBP; requires cuff initialization + ~100 features.
Frontiers Med Technol 2024 (10.3389/fmedt.2024.1464473) PPG cuffless bracelet — SBP mean diff 0.5 (SD 7.8) mmHg sitting, -2.4 (10.1) supine, -0.6 (12.5) standing; accuracy posture-dependent.
General cuffless-PPG reviews (PMC7141397, PMC6603632) — PWA on wrist PPG is dominant approach; accuracy of cuffless devices remains uncertain pending regulatory recommendations.

variable: 보행 속도 (Gait speed / usual walking speed)
what_it_measures: Usual-pace overground walking velocity (m/s), typically over 4-6 m; a global integrator of cardiorespiratory fitness, lower-limb function, and neuromuscular health. Easy, cheap, reproducible vital sign.
bp_relationship: Inverse / protective: faster gait speed predicts LOWER incident hypertension and LOWER CVD/all-cause mortality; slower gait associates with HIGHER arterial stiffness and worse CV outcomes. Direction: faster gait → better BP/CV profile. Caveat: largely observational; reverse causation (vascular disease slows gait) is plausible.
references: Veronese N et al. 2018, J Am Med Dir Assoc (JAMDA) — meta-analysis, 45 studies / ~101,945 adults (mean age 72.2y, median FU 5.4y): each 0.1 m/s SLOWER gait = +12% all-cause mortality (HR 1.12, 95%CI 1.09-1.14) and +8% CVD (HR 1.08, 95%CI 1.03-1.13).

LaCroix AZ / WHI 2020, Hypertension (HYPERTENSIONAHA.120.15839) — 83,435 postmenopausal women, mean 11y FU, 38,230 incident treated-HTN cases: faster usual walking speed inversely associated with incident treated hypertension; HRs by pace ~1.00 (<2 mph, ref), 0.95, 0.86, 0.79 (>4 mph); significant after adjusting for baseline BP and walking volume.

Lee et al. 2025, Geriatr Gerontol Int (ggi.70225) — Korean cohort n=1890 older adults: high vs low walking-speed group had 46% lower odds of hypertension (OR 0.52, 95%CI 0.29-0.94); stronger in low-insulin-resistance subgroup (OR 0.35, 95%CI 0.14-0.85).

Mobility speed study 2021, PubMed 34267074 / PMC8734633 — longitudinal: 4-m walk score inversely related to incident hypertension (HR 0.07, 95%CI 0.01-0.47, p=0.007); slower Timed-Up-and-Go positively associated (HR 1.15).

Afilalo J et al. 2010, J Am Coll Cardiol — slow gait speed (frailty marker) post-MI/cardiac surgery: >3-fold increase in morbidity/mortality; STS database (JAHA 2019) HR 2.16 per 0.1 m/s slower for 1-yr mortality after cardiac surgery.

what_it_measures: Distance covered per stride (or step); spatial gait parameter reflecting lower-limb power, balance confidence, and neuromuscular integrity. Strongly co-varies with gait speed.
bp_relationship: Inverse / protective: SHORTER stride length predicts adverse clinical events and disability; reduced step length is a frailty feature linked to higher CV risk. Direct BP/stiffness associations are sparse and mostly mediated through gait speed and frailty rather than a standalone BP mechanism.
references: Stride Length Predicts Adverse Clinical Events — Systematic Review & Meta-analysis 2021, PMC8235531: short stride length predicted major adverse events (pooled OR 1.36) and physical disability (OR 1.26) in older adults.

Frailty/gait studies (Frontiers Med 2026, 3389/fmed.2026.1785926) — frail older adults show shorter step length, prolonged double-support, and reduced gait speed vs robust; frailty phenotype associates with higher CV risk.

Tudor-Locke / steps & cadence 2021, PubMed 34798157 (steps/day & peak cadence vs arterial stiffness) — ambulatory volume & intensity (which scale with stride/cadence) inversely associated with arterial stiffness; peak cadence 68-86 steps/min linked to lower BP and better metabolic markers.
NOTE: Most stride-length evidence validates against falls/disability/frailty; BP and PWV links are indirect, mediated by overall mobility and vascular risk-factor burden.

variable: 이중 지지 시간 (Double-support time / gait variability)
what_it_measures: Proportion/duration of the gait cycle with both feet on the ground (a stability/cautious-gait marker); plus step-to-step variability (CV of stride time, length, double-support) reflecting gait control and CNS/cardiovascular integrity.
bp_relationship: Positive (longer double-support / higher variability = worse): hypertensive individuals walk slower with LONGER double-support time and GREATER gait variability vs normotensives. Increased variability marks frailty/fall risk and tracks with CV disease burden. Direction: longer double-support/higher variability → higher BP/CV-risk phenotype, but mechanism is indirect (shared vascular-neural aging).
references: Determining characteristics of gait variability at preferred speed in hypertensive vs normotensive participants 2023, J Clin Hypertens (S2213-3981(23)00131-8) — hypertensive participants: slower speed, longer double-support time, and significantly greater gait variability than normotensives.

Medical, Sensorimotor & Cognitive Factors Associated with Gait Variability — longitudinal population study, PMC6305368: baseline CVD increased rate of change of step-length variability; weaker quadriceps increased step-width variability change.

Frailty & gait variability (Front Med 2026, 3389/fmed.2026.1785926; PMC3034676) — prolonged double-support and greater stride variability characterize frailty and predict falls; stride variability may detect fall risk more sensitively than speed.

PAD gait studies (BMC Geriatr 2018, 10.1186/s12877-018-0727-1) — peripheral artery disease: greater step/stride times, altered stance/swing — vascular disease directly perturbs temporal gait parameters.

variable: 보행 비대칭성 / 안정성 (Gait asymmetry / walking steadiness/stability)
what_it_measures: Left-right asymmetry in step length/time and swing/stance (spatial & temporal), plus walking steadiness/regularity and stability indices (e.g., regularity from accelerometry, Apple-style walking steadiness). Markers of motor control, fall risk, and limb-specific perfusion.
bp_relationship: Indirect, disease-mediated: gait asymmetry/instability is elevated in PAD (correlates with ankle-brachial index and ABI asymmetry — a peripheral vascular/atherosclerosis readout) and in stroke survivors. No clean direct BP-magnitude relationship; rather, vascular disease (atherosclerosis/PAD, cerebrovascular events from hypertension) produces asymmetry/instability. Direction: greater asymmetry/lower steadiness → greater underlying vascular/CV disease burden.
references: Effect of PAD & intermittent claudication on gait regularity and symmetry 2022, J Biomech (PubMed 35759975 / S0021-9290(22)00248-2): gait asymmetry increased after claudication onset; ML asymmetry and vertical stride regularity correlated with ankle-brachial index (ABI) and ABI asymmetry.
Gait pattern in patients with PAD 2018, BMC Geriatrics (PMC5819174): reduced gait speed, step/stride regularity vs controls; affected limb longer swing, shorter stance.

Patterson KK et al. — Gait Asymmetry in Community-Ambulating Stroke Survivors (ResearchGate 5624524) & systematic review 2025 (S0966-6362(25)00223-1): spatial (step length) and temporal (swing time) paretic/non-paretic asymmetry common post-stroke (a hypertension-driven cerebrovascular outcome).
Walking Stability and Risk of Falls 2023, PMC10135799 — instability indices (SPPB, POMA-G) inversely relate to fall risk; gait/balance disorders predict falls better than other domains.
estimated Pulse Wave Velocity & balance function in US adults 2025, J Hum Hypertens (s41371-025-01062-0) — higher ePWV (arterial stiffness surrogate) associated with worse balance function.

what_it_measures: Speed/ability to ascend stairs (e.g., flights climbed, 60-second step test, standardized stair-climbing test) — a practical surrogate for cardiorespiratory fitness (VO2max), leg power, and CV reserve.
bp_relationship: Inverse / protective as a fitness marker: greater stair-climbing activity/capacity associates with LOWER CVD and all-cause mortality and better CV risk-factor profile. Acutely, stair climbing raises HR, stroke volume, and systolic BP (training stimulus); chronically improves fitness and lowers risk. Direction: higher stair-climbing capacity → better CV/BP profile. Caveat: reverse causation (fitter people climb more).
references: Paddock S et al. 2024, Eur J Prev Cardiol (zwae175.405) — systematic review/meta-analysis, 9 studies / 480,520 participants (pooled 5 studies, 455,649): stair climbing associated with 24% lower all-cause mortality and 39% lower CVD mortality; pooled CV-mortality RR 0.65 (95%CI 0.50-0.83).

Daily stair climbing, disease susceptibility & ASCVD — prospective cohort 2023 (S0021-9150(23)05221-8): climbing >5 flights (~50 steps)/day associated with lower ASCVD risk independent of genetic susceptibility.
Suita Study 2024, PMC11111291 — stair climbing associated with favorable modifiable CVD risk factors (incl. blood pressure/metabolic markers).

Standardized stair-climbing test as CRF surrogate (PMC10887637, PMC12384133) — validates stair climbing as a simple cardiorespiratory-fitness measure; stair climbing acutely increases HR, stroke volume, systolic BP.

variable: 일어서기 시간 / 좌식 시간 (Standing/sitting & sedentary time)
what_it_measures: Time spent sedentary (sitting), uninterrupted sitting bouts, and standing time (sit-to-stand transitions, Timed-Up-and-Go captures rise capacity). Reflects daily activity exposure and vascular response to immobility.
bp_relationship: Positive (sedentary = worse): MORE sitting / longer uninterrupted sitting acutely RAISES blood pressure and aortic PWV (arterial stiffness) and is dose-dependently associated with incident hypertension and CVD. Replacing sitting with movement lowers BP, but merely STANDING (without ambulating) does NOT reliably lower BP and excessive standing may harm. Direction: more sedentary time → higher BP/stiffness/CV risk; light activity (not just standing) → lower BP.
references: Barone Gibbs B et al. 2024, Circulation (RESET-BP RCT, CIRCULATIONAHA.123.068564) — reducing sedentary behavior lowered BP in desk workers; companion analysis (WVU 2024) — increasing standing ~1 h/day for 3 months did NOT reduce BP or arterial stiffness.

Prolonged Sitting Induces Elevated BP in Healthy Young Men — RCT crossover 2024, PMC10981358; and Single Bout of Prolonged Sitting Augments Very Short-Term BP Variability 2024, Am J Hypertens (PMC11322278) — acute sitting raises BP / BP variability.
Estimated PWV tracking arterial response to prolonged sitting 2022, PMC9785284 — ~3 h sitting increased aortic PWV (aortic stiffness) in healthy young adults.

Dose-response sedentary time & mortality/CVD meta-analyses — Ekelund U et al. 2019 (PubMed 31434697); JAHA 2021 (10.1161/JAHA.121.023845): higher sedentary time → incident atrial fibrillation (HR 1.11, 95%CI 1.01-1.21) and MI (HR 1.15, 95%CI 1.00-1.32); sitting time dose-response HRs rising to ~2.63 in highest category; each +1 h/day sitting HR ~1.04 for mortality.
Sedentary Behavior & Light PA vs Brachial/Central BP in hypertensives 2016, PMC4696789 — less sedentary + more light activity → lower BP (no change in stiffness/autonomic tone).

what_it_measures: Wrist (distal) skin temperature from a wrist-worn thermistor; a non-invasive surrogate of peripheral skin blood flow and cutaneous vasodilation/vasoconstriction. Rising wrist temp = peripheral vasodilation (heat loss, lower total peripheral resistance); falling wrist temp = vasoconstriction. The wrist-temperature rhythm is a validated marker of the circadian system (stable phase relationship with dim-light melatonin onset) and of autonomic balance, thermoregulation and sleep propensity. The distal-minus-proximal gradient (DPG) is used as a finer index of distal skin blood flow.
bp_relationship: INVERSE / negative, mechanistically driven by peripheral vasoconstriction–vasodilation (total peripheral resistance). Higher wrist/distal skin temperature -> lower SBP/DBP. Across the day the BP rhythm is approximately a mirror image of the distal skin temperature (and DPG) rhythm. Low DAYTIME distal skin blood flow (cooler distal temp by day) predicts blunted nocturnal BP dipping (non-dipping), so the wrist-temperature circadian profile can screen dipper vs non-dipper status. Mechanism: nocturnal parasympathetic activation + supine position -> distal vasodilation -> heat loss + BP fall.
references: Wang et al. 2022, Hypertension (AHA) 'Inverse Association of Skin Temperature With Ambulatory Blood Pressure...' (Nara Medical Univ.) — n=584 older adults, 48h ABPM + skin temp (30,711 daytime / 17,382 nighttime readings, Oct–Mar). Distal (wrist+ankle) and proximal (abdomen) skin temp inversely associated with daytime SBP: −4.27 mmHg (95% CI −4.58 to −3.96) and −2.74 mmHg per SD of skin temp; associations also significant at night; skin temp mediated BP responses to ambient temperature (path analysis). PMID 35574922; DOI 10.1161/HYPERTENSIONAHA.122.19190

Blázquez/Sarabia/Madrid et al. 2012, Chronobiology International 29(6):747 'Wrist Skin Temperature, Motor Activity, and Body Position as Determinants of the Circadian Pattern of Blood Pressure' — wrist temp negatively correlated with SBP/DBP; 78.6% concordance between observed BP dips and BP pattern predicted from wrist-temperature rhythm. PMID 22734575

Zaslavsky/… (van Marken Lichtenbelt group) 2012, Chronobiology International 29(9):1180 'Diurnal Blood Pressure Variations Are Associated With Changes in Distal–Proximal Skin Temperature Gradient' — n=51, 24h BP + distal & proximal skin temp; diurnal mean arterial pressure pattern was nearly a mirror image of the distal–proximal gradient (distal skin blood flow). PMID 23003124

'Non-dipping blood pressure pattern is related to an increase in daytime distal skin temperature' 2014, Sleep Medicine (ScienceDirect S1389945713016687) — low daytime distal skin blood flow predicts sleep-induced BP dipping; increased daytime distal skin temp associates with non-dipping.

Sarabia, Ortiz-Tudela, Rol, Madrid et al. 2008, Physiology & Behavior 95(4):570 'Circadian rhythm of wrist temperature in normal-living subjects: a candidate of new index of the circadian system' (Univ. Murcia) — foundational validation of wrist temperature as a circadian/autonomic marker; acrophase & mesor most robust, amplitude most masked. PMID 18761026

Martinez-Nicolas, Ortiz-Tudela, Madrid, Rol 2013, PLOS ONE 8(4):e61142 'Uncovering Different Masking Factors on Wrist Skin Temperature Rhythm in Free-Living Subjects' — quantifies masking (activity, body position, ambient temp) on wrist-temp rhythm. PMID 23577201

what_it_measures: Personal light exposure (daytime bright-light dose, morning light timing, light-at-night) from wrist-worn light sensors, and environmental daylight hours / photoperiod (seasonality). Two conceptually different exposures: (a) acute solar/UV dose on skin; (b) circadian light signal to the eye that entrains the master clock and the BP rhythm. Daytime daylight = strong entraining signal (sunlight ~tens of thousands of lux vs weak indoor light); light-at-night = circadian disruptor.
bp_relationship: DUAL / direction depends on timing. (1) Acute photobiology: solar UV-A mobilizes cutaneous nitric-oxide stores -> systemic vasodilation -> LOWER SBP (independent of temperature). (2) Circadian: adequate DAYTIME / MORNING bright light reinforces the nocturnal BP dip and supports lower daytime BP; LACK of morning light and LIGHT-AT-NIGHT blunt dipping, raise nighttime BP/morning surge and CV risk. Seasonality measured as DAYLIGHT HOURS is an independent predictor: nighttime SBP positively related to daylight hours and morning BP surge negatively related to daylight hours (i.e., long photoperiod/summer -> higher nighttime SBP, smaller morning surge), distinct from the temperature effect on daytime SBP. Net wintertime (short daylight + cold) elevation of daytime BP.
references: Weller/Feelisch et al. 2020, JAHA 9(5):e013837 'Does Incident Solar Ultraviolet Radiation Lower Blood Pressure?' — large N. American chronic hemodialysis cohort; higher incident solar UV associated with lower SBP after adjusting for ambient temperature; supports UV->cutaneous NO->lower BP mechanism. PMC7335547

Liedtke/… 2023, Scientific Reports 13 (Nature) 'Low-dose daylight exposure induces nitric oxide release and maintains cell viability in vitro' — mechanistic support that daylight-level exposure triggers NO release. DOI 10.1038/s41598-023-43653-2

Modesti et al. 2013, Hypertension 61(4):908 'Seasonal Blood Pressure Changes: An Independent Relationship With Temperature and Daylight Hours' — n=1,897 hypertension-unit patients, ABPM + personal environmental temperature logger; daytime SBP negatively related to personal temperature, NIGHTTIME SBP positively related to DAYLIGHT HOURS, and MORNING BP SURGE negatively related to daylight hours; temperature and seasonality (daylight hours) are two independent ABPM predictors. PMID 23381792

Stergiou/Kollias et al. (reviewed in Narita & Kario 2021/2023, Hypertension Research) seasonal-variation meta-analyses — winter–summer difference in daytime home BP 6.1/3.1 mmHg (SBP/DBP) and in daytime ambulatory BP 3.4/2.1 mmHg; winter daytime BP elevation; summer nighttime BP elevation. DOI 10.1038/s41440-021-00732-z

Windred/Cain et al. 2025, JAMA Network Open 8(10):e2539031 'Light Exposure at Night and Cardiovascular Disease Incidence' — UK Biobank, n=88,905 (>40y), wrist light sensors (~13 million hours, 1 wk), 9.5y follow-up. Brightest-night group (91–100th pct) vs darkest (0–50th): higher risk of coronary artery disease (aHR 1.32), MI (1.47), heart failure (1.56), atrial fibrillation (1.32), stroke (1.28). PMID 41129148

'Morning light exposure: a potential modifier of cardiovascular risk factor' 2025, Medical Hypotheses (ScienceDirect S0306987725000933) — argues reduced morning light lessens the overnight BP drop and lowers night melatonin, raising CV risk; morning bright light advances circadian rhythm.

Brown et al. 2022, PLOS Biology 20(3):e3001571 'Recommendations for daytime, evening, and nighttime indoor light exposure...' — consensus thresholds (≥250 lux melanopic EDI daytime) underpinning circadian-light dosing used in BP/circadian work.

what_it_measures: Sleep duration, subjective quality (PSQI), efficiency, timing/midsleep (chronotype) and continuity, from self-report, actigraphy or wearables. Captures both the quantity and the restorativeness/continuity of sleep and the sleep period that defines the nocturnal BP window for dipping calculation.
bp_relationship: Generally INVERSE for quantity/quality vs BP and dipping: SHORT or POOR sleep -> HIGHER BP, higher incident hypertension, and BLUNTED nocturnal dipping / non-dipping (sympathetic over-activity, reduced parasympathetic tone at night). Better subjective sleep quality -> more likely dipper. Sleep also defines the morning-surge/dip windows. Evidence is strongest for short duration and subjective quality; some OBJECTIVE measures (actigraphic duration/efficiency) show weaker/null links to non-dipping (CARDIA). OSA (a sleep disorder) strongly raises non-dipping prevalence.
references: Lo, Woo et al. 2018, Journal of Clinical Hypertension 20(3):592 'Subjective sleep quality, blood pressure, and hypertension: a meta-analysis' — 29 articles/45,041 patients; poor sleep quality associated with hypertension (OR 1.48, P=.01); highest sleep-disturbance tertile OR 1.44 (1.00–1.64); poor sleepers higher SBP (MD 4.37) and DBP (MD 1.25) (NS). Notes dippers have better subjective sleep quality than non-dippers. PMID 29457339

Gangwisch et al. 2006, Hypertension (NHANES I, n≈4,810, 8–10y) — sleep ≤5 h/night raised incident hypertension (adjusted HR 1.32, 95% CI 1.02–1.71 vs 7–8h); ages 32–59 with ≤5h ~60% more likely to develop hypertension.

2024 meta-analysis, PLOS ONE 19, 'Association between sleep duration and hypertension incidence: systematic review and meta-analysis of cohort studies' — 16 cohorts, 1,044,035 participants; short sleep -> hypertension HR 1.07 (1.06–1.09), and HR 1.11 (1.08–1.14) for <5 h; women more vulnerable (HR 1.07, 1.04–1.09). DOI 10.1371/journal.pone.0307120

Full/… 2020, JAHA 9(7):e015062 'Association of Sleep Characteristics With Nocturnal Hypertension and Nondipping Blood Pressure in the CARDIA Study' — Year-30 exam, 24h ABPM + PSQI + actigraphy; NO association of global sleep quality, habitual duration, or sleep efficiency with nocturnal HTN or nondipping SBP; later midsleep time (chronotype) associated with LOWER prevalence ratio for nondipping SBP. Nocturnal HTN = asleep SBP ≥120 or DBP ≥70. DOI 10.1161/JAHA.119.015062

Cuspidi et al. 2019, J Clinical Hypertension / Cuspidi meta-analysis 'Blood Pressure Non-Dipping and Obstructive Sleep Apnea Syndrome: A Meta-Analysis' — OSA markedly increases prevalence of non-dipping pattern on 24h ABPM. PMC6780266

Korean hypertension registry study 2023 (PMC10391961) 'Clinical and life style factors related to nighttime BP, nighttime dipping and phenotypes' — sleep duration and quality associated with nighttime SBP; sleep quality associated with extent of nighttime SBP dipping.

Kario et al. 2003, Circulation 107:1401 'Morning Surge in BP as a Predictor of Silent and Clinical Cerebrovascular Disease in Elderly Hypertensives' (and JAMP Study, Hypertension 2020) — morning surge (mean SBP 2h after waking minus surrounding-trough SBP) predicts stroke; ~+10 mmHg surge ≈ +22% stroke risk. Links sleep/wake transition physiology to BP-derived risk. PMID 12642361

bp_relationship: Inverse: higher daily step volume associated with lower SBP, DBP, MAP and pulse pressure, and lower MACE in hypertensives. Mechanism — chronic adaptation lowering peripheral resistance, improved endothelial/NO vasodilation, better baroreflex, reduced sympathetic tone; dose-dependent up to ~8,000–10,000 steps.
references: del Pozo Cruz et al. 2024, Eur J Prev Cardiol — UK Biobank hypertensives (n≈32,192): each +1,000 steps/day vs ~2,300 baseline ~17% lower MACE risk.
Chen et al. 2024, JMIR — higher wearable daily steps associated with lower SBP/DBP/MAP/pulse pressure in older adults; greater step variability linked to higher BP.

bp_relationship: Inverse, dose-responsive: greater active energy / MVPA lowers resting BP and incident hypertension via chronic vascular remodeling + repeated acute post-exercise hypotension. Caveat: wrist active-kcal carries large error (MAPE often >20%).
references: Stamatakis et al. (ProPASS) 2025, Circulation (n=14,761) — reallocating time toward exercise-like activity yields clinically meaningful BP reductions (≥2 mmHg SBP).
Liu et al. 2017, Hypertension — ~6% lower hypertension risk per 10 MET-h/week of leisure-time activity.

bp_relationship: Positive (opposite to activity metrics): higher resting/basal metabolic rate associated with higher BP independent of body size — elevated resting rate-pressure product, greater sympathetic activity, lower insulin sensitivity.
references: Weyer et al. 2000, Hypertension — obese hypertensives show significantly higher RMR vs BMI-matched normotensives.
MR study 2023, Sci Rep — genetically higher BMR causally raises heart failure / aortic aneurysm risk (adverse CV direction).

bp_relationship: Inverse and intensity-graded: higher intensity lowers BP more per unit time; reductions scale with MET-minutes. Stronger acute post-exercise hypotension + chronic drop in peripheral resistance/arterial stiffness; HIIT especially effective.
references: Network meta-analysis 2023, Sci Rep — HIIT ~690 MET-min/week most effective for SBP (−5.07 mmHg); ~830 MET-min/week best for DBP (−4.42 mmHg).
Pescatello et al. (ACSM) — regular aerobic exercise lowers BP ~5–7 mmHg in hypertensives.

bp_relationship: Inverse: accumulating exercise duration (~150 min/week) lowers resting SBP/DBP. Acute — each 20–60 min bout >40% VO2peak triggers post-exercise hypotension up to ~24 h; chronic — sustained ambulatory BP reduction.
references: Resistant-HTN RCT 2021 (PMC8340008) — 12 wk of 3×40-min/week reduced 24-h and daytime ambulatory SBP/DBP.
Halliwill 2001, post-exercise hypotension review — PEH after submaximal dynamic exercise persists for hours (up to ~13 h).

bp_relationship: Inverse, energy-driven: greater distance lowers incident hypertension and SBP/DBP; at matched energy expenditure walking and running give comparable benefit.
references: Williams & Thompson 2013, ATVB (n≈49,000) — running cut incident hypertension 4.2% and walking 7.2% per MET-h/day; comparable at equal energy.
Walking intervention 2018 (PMC6119598) — 6-month guided walking lowered SBP up to −21.3 mmHg in those with baseline SBP >160.

bp_relationship: Inverse: habitual stair climbing lowers SBP and arterial stiffness, increases leg strength; more flights/day associated with lower CVD risk. Brief vigorous weight-bearing load reduces arterial stiffness (key SBP mediator).
references: Hong et al. 2018 (Korean postmenopausal stage-2 HTN) — 12-wk stair-climbing reduced arterial stiffness and BP; stiffness drop partly explained SBP improvement.
Tulane Mediterranean cohort — climbing >5 flights/day associated with ~20% lower ASCVD risk.
